explanation_md: `int32_t` is exactly 32 bits = 4 bytes, and `uint64_t` is exactly 64 bits = 8 bytes. These are guaranteed by the fixed-width integer specification — unlike `int` or `long`, which can vary by platform. `sizeof` returns the size in bytes, so the output is `4` then `8`.

`int8_t` is typically a `typedef` for `signed char`. When passed to `std::cout`, the stream treats it as a character rather than an integer, so it prints the ASCII character for 65, which is `A`. To print the numeric value, you would need to cast it to `int` with `static_cast<int>(x)` (covered in lesson 4.12).

prompt_md: Which header must you include to use the fixed-width integer types like `int32_t` and `uint64_t`?
explanation_md: The fixed-width integer types defined by C99/C++11 live in `<cstdint>` (the C++ version of the C header `<stdint.h>`). `<stdint>` and `<types>` and `<integer>` are not standard headers. Many other headers pull in `<cstdint>` as a side effect, but relying on that is fragile — include it directly.

prompt_md: Why is `std::size_t` used by the standard library for sizes and lengths instead of plain `int`?

options.a: Because `int` cannot hold values larger than 32,767
options.b: Because `std::size_t` is signed, which prevents overflow
options.c: Because `std::size_t` is wide enough to represent the size of any object on the current platform, and it is unsigned so negative sizes are not expressible
options.d: Because `int` is slower than `std::size_t` on 64-bit platforms

explanation_md: `std::size_t` is defined to be large enough to hold the byte size of any object the platform can allocate. On a 64-bit system that may be 64 bits wide. It is unsigned because negative sizes make no sense. Option (a) is wrong — `int` is at least 16 bits but usually 32. Option (b) is wrong — `size_t` is unsigned, not signed. Option (d) is a common misconception; performance is not the reason.

options.b: Subtracting 1 from `std::size_t` value 0 wraps around to the maximum unsigned value, not -1
options.c: `last_index` should be declared as `int` to hold the result
options.d: The program will throw a runtime exception because sizes cannot be negative

explanation_md: `std::size_t` is an unsigned type. Subtracting 1 from 0 does not produce -1 — it wraps around to the maximum value representable by `size_t` (e.g., 18,446,744,073,709,551,615 on a 64-bit platform). This is silent and produces a meaningless huge number. The fix is to check that `buffer_size > 0` before subtracting, or use a signed type for index arithmetic.
